topic.name: Races and Games
topic.description: Running races, dead heats, head starts, and games of skill involving winning margins and proportional speeds. Estimated reading 20 min; practice 35 min.

theory.introduction: Races and Games problems relate distances covered, speeds, and winning margins. When A beats B by d metres in a race of L metres, A runs L while B runs (L − d) in the same time, giving speed ratio L : (L − d). Games of skill ask how many consecutive wins are needed under a point system.
theory.explanation:
CONCEPT EXPLANATION

In a race of L metres, if A beats B by d metres, then when A completes L metres, B has run (L − d) metres. Since time is equal, speed ratio A : B = L : (L − d). Similarly, if A beats B by t seconds, compute distances each runs in time t to relate speeds.

A head start of h metres means the advantaged runner begins h metres ahead. For games of skill: if A needs a wins and B needs b wins, and A wins m games while B wins n games with m < a and n < b, find remaining games or probability as stated.

REAL-LIFE APPLICATIONS

Track athletics handicapping, relay team selection, tournament knockout formats, and gaming tournament rules use these proportional reasoning patterns.

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Using d/L as the speed ratio instead of L/(L−d).
2) Ignoring head start when computing effective race length.
3) Confusing beating by time with beating by distance without conversion.
4) Adding head start to winning margin incorrectly.
5) Miscounting games needed in skill-game problems.

IMPORTANT NOTES

• Same time ⇒ distance ratio equals speed ratio.
• Head start reduces the distance the favoured runner must cover.
• Dead heat means both reach the finish together — speeds equal over that distance.
• In circular track problems, first meeting uses relative speed on lap length.
• Always identify who runs full distance L when beaten by d metres.

formulas.title: Beating by distance
formulas.content: If A beats B by d m in a race of L m, then Speed A : Speed B = L : (L − d)

formulas.title: Beating by time
formulas.content: If A finishes L metres and B is d metres behind at that instant, then B has run (L − d) metres in the same time

formulas.title: Head start
formulas.content: With head start h, the other runner must cover L metres while the favoured runner covers (L − h) metres to win

formulas.title: Time-based speeds
formulas.content: If A runs L1 in time T and B runs L2 in the same time T, then Speed A : Speed B = L1 : L2

formulas.title: Games of skill
formulas.content: If A needs a wins and B needs b wins, after A wins m and B wins n games, A needs (a − m) more wins and B needs (b − n) more wins

formulas.title: Circular track first meeting
formulas.content: Time to first meet (same direction) = Track length / |Speed A − Speed B|

shortcutTips.title: L and L−d
shortcutTips.tip: When A beats B by d in L metres, write speed ratio as L : (L − d) immediately.

shortcutTips.title: Head start first
shortcutTips.tip: Subtract the head start from the race length for the runner who gets it before comparing speeds.

shortcutTips.title: Time beat conversion
shortcutTips.tip: If beaten by t seconds, find how far B runs while A completes L using equal-time distance ratios.

shortcutTips.title: Equal time principle
shortcutTips.tip: All race ratio problems assume both run for the same time when the winner finishes.

shortcutTips.title: Games remaining
shortcutTips.tip: In skill games, subtract games already won from the target wins for each player.

shortcutTips.title: Check ratio direction
shortcutTips.tip: Always put the faster runner's distance on the left side of the speed ratio.

solvedExamples.problem: A beats B by 20 metres in a 200 m race. In a 100 m race, by how many metres will A beat B?
steps: When A runs 100 m, B runs 100 × 9/10 = 90 m.

solvedExamples.problem: A beats B by 10 m in a 100 m race. If A runs at 5 m/s, find B's speed.
steps: When A runs 100 m, B runs 90 m in the same time.

solvedExamples.problem: A can run 1 km in 5 minutes and B in 6 minutes. By what distance does A beat B in a 1 km race?
solvedExamples.solution: A beats B by 166⅔ metres (approximately 167 m).
steps: When A completes 1000 m, time elapsed = 5 minutes.

solvedExamples.problem: A and B play a game where the first to win 3 games wins. If A has won 2 games and B has won 1, how many more games must A win to win the match?
steps: A needs 3 wins total and already has 2.

exercise.title: General Questions
exercise.description: MCQ practice on race margins, head starts, speed ratios, and games of skill.

questions.question: A beats B by 10 m in a 100 m race. What is the ratio of their speeds?
A beats B by 20 m in a 200 m race. In a 100 m race, by how many metres will A beat B?
questions.explanation: Ratio 10:9. B runs 90 m when A runs 100 m. Beat = 10 m.

questions.question: In a 500 m race, A beats B by 50 m. Find the ratio of speeds of A and B.
A runs 1000 m in 4 minutes. B runs 900 m in the same time. By how many metres will A beat B in a 1000 m race?
questions.explanation: When A runs 1000 m, B runs 900 m. Beat = 100 m.

questions.question: A beats B by 15 m in a 300 m race. In a 200 m race, A will beat B by:
A can run 1 km in 5 min and B in 6 min. By what distance does A beat B in a 1 km race?
questions.explanation: In 5 min B runs 5000/6 m. Beat = 1000 − 833⅓ = 166⅔ m.

questions.question: In a 1 km race, A gives B a start of 100 m and still beats him by 50 m. What is the ratio of their speeds?
A beats B by 10 m in a 100 m race. If B's speed is 9 m/s, find A's speed.
Two runners start together on a 400 m circular track and run in the same direction at 8 m/s and 6 m/s. After how many seconds do they first meet?
questions.explanation: Relative speed = 2 m/s. Time to first meet = 400/2 = 200 seconds.

questions.question: A and B contest a game; the first to score 5 wins wins. If A has 3 wins and B has 2, how many more wins does A need?
In a 800 m race, A beats B by 40 m and B beats C by 20 m. By how many metres does A beat C?
questions.explanation: When A runs 800 m, B runs 760 m. When B runs 760 m, C runs 760×780/800 = 741 m. A beats C by 59 m.

questions.question: A beats B by 25 m in a 250 m race. B's time for the race is 50 seconds. Find A's time.
questions.explanation: B runs 225 m in 50 s. A runs 250 m at ratio 250/225 × 50 = 500/9 ≈ 55.56? Ratio speed A:B=250:225=10:9. Time inverse: A time = 50×9/10 = 45 s.

questions.question: In a game, A needs 4 wins and B needs 5 wins. After 6 games A leads 4–2. Who has won the match?
questions.explanation: A reached 4 wins — A wins the match.

questions.question: A gives B a head start of 10 m in a 100 m race and beats him by 10 m. Find the ratio of their speeds.
questions.explanation: A runs 100 m. B is at 90 m when A finishes (beat by 10 from 100 m mark), so B ran 80 m from start. Ratio = 100:80 = 5:4.

questions.question: A and B run at 5 m/s and 4 m/s on a 300 m circular track in the same direction. After how many seconds do they first meet?
In a 1500 m race, A beats B by 150 m and B beats C by 100 m. By how much does A beat C?
A beats B by 12 m in a 120 m race. What distance does B cover when A runs 80 m?
In a 100 m race, A can give B a start of 10 m and still win by 5 m. Find the ratio of speeds of A and B.
A runs twice as fast as B. By what distance will A beat B in a 400 m race?
questions.explanation: When A runs 400 m, B runs 200 m. Beat = 200 m.

questions.question: First to win 3 games wins. Score is 2–1 in favour of A. What is the maximum number of additional games that can be played?
questions.explanation: A needs 1 more win; B needs 2. Maximum games = 1 + 2 = 3? If B wins next two then A wins: could be 3 more. Max additional = 3 if B wins 2 then A wins 1. Actually max = 2 (B wins twice to 2-3, then A wins). Score 2-1, B needs 2, A needs 1. Max games = 2+1-1 = 2 additional if alternating. Answer B (2).

questions.question: A beats B by 30 m in a 600 m race. In a 300 m race, A beats B by:
A and B start a 1000 m race together. A runs at 8 m/s. When A finishes, B has 100 m to go. Find B's speed.
questions.explanation: A's time = 1000/8 = 125 s. B runs 900 m in 125 s → 7.2 m/s.

questions.question: In a 400 m race, the ratio of speeds of A and B is 5 : 4. By how many metres does A beat B?
